one_line_takeaway: AGEA is the first agentic, query-efficient black-box attack that reconstructs up to ~90%+ of a GraphRAG system's hidden entity-relation knowledge graph with high precision under a fixed 1000-query budget, showing structured GraphRAG retrieval is highly vulnerable to graph-level theft.
key_finding: AGEA substantially outperforms all four prior black-box RAG-extraction baselines under identical 1000-query budgets on both GraphRAG systems, recovering up to ~90%+ of nodes and edges while keeping high precision, and it closes the node-vs-edge gap that cripples prior attacks (baselines extract many nodes but miss relations). The strongest baseline differs by system (PIDE on LightRAG, TGTB on M-GraphRAG), indicating different GraphRAG constructions expose different attack surfaces. Ablations show each design choice matters: adaptive query strategy beats explore-only and exploit-only; the LLM filter trades a tiny leakage cost for a large precision gain (removing it drops node precision sharply); the DeepSeek victim backbone gives the best relation faithfulness; and the structured 'universal extraction command' materially raises leakage over naive 'repeat the context' commands. Scalability: leakage stays high on small/medium graphs but degrades on the largest graph (Novel, 20 books, |V|=8259) under a fixed budget, since the graph grows faster than the query count.
core_contribution: The paper formalizes and attacks a new privacy threat: query-efficient, black-box reconstruction of the hidden entity-relation knowledge graph inside a GraphRAG system under a fixed query budget. Its own contribution (distinct from cited prior work like Liu et al. 2025, which only showed GraphRAG can leak individual retrieved subgraph fragments) is AGEA (Agentic Graph Extraction Attack), described as the first agentic graph-extraction attack framework for GraphRAG. AGEA runs an iterative closed feedback loop with four components: an adaptive attack-query generator Q, a two-stage extraction-filter module F, and two persistent memory modules (Graph Memory M_G and Query Memory M_Q). The novelty over prior RAG extraction attacks is that it targets reconstructing the whole latent graph (a reusable, queryable artifact) rather than leaking chunk/document-level text, and does so query-efficiently via a novelty-guided exploration-exploitation policy.
threat_model: Strict black-box, budget-constrained adversary. The attacker has no access to the GraphRAG system's internals (retriever, underlying graph G, base LLM parameters) and can only submit a sequence of queries {q(t)} for t=1..T and observe the returned free-form text responses R(t)=LLM(q(t), G_q(t)). The attacker never directly observes the retrieved subgraph. Goal: recover the system's internal graph G = (V entities, E relations) with high coverage of both nodes and edges while using as few queries as possible (query efficiency). Each constructed KG (built by M-GraphRAG or LightRAG over the same corpus) is treated as a protected/proprietary asset to be stolen. The paper explicitly does NOT model deployment-time defenses (rate limiting, query monitoring, response filtering, adaptive multi-turn detection); it characterizes risk in a clean black-box setting.
limitations: (1) Depends on eliciting structured outputs: if a victim enforces paragraph-only responses, aggressively rewrites outputs, or suppresses explicit entity-relation formatting, regex-based Stage-A extraction reliability drops. (2) Threat-model scope is defense-free: no query monitoring, rate limiting, query rewriting, response filtering, or multi-turn extraction detection is modeled, so results are a clean upper bound rather than defense-aware attack-defense dynamics. (3) Coverage limited to two GraphRAG systems (M-GraphRAG, LightRAG) and a handful of domain datasets; extraction behavior may vary with other graph-construction pipelines, retrieval designs, and corpus quality. (4) Leakage falls under fixed budgets as graph size grows (large-scale efficiency is limited). (5) Reviewer note: two adaptive baselines (CopyBreakRAG, IKEA) were re-implemented by the authors from paper descriptions because original code was unavailable, which may under-represent their true strength.

datasets_used: Medical (GraphRAG-Bench / Xiao et al. 2025; clinical guidelines; |V|=1415, |E|=2334)
quantitative_results: M-GraphRAG Medical, T=1000: AGEA Leak(N)=87.09, Leak(E)=80.16, Prec(N)=87.09, Prec(E)=61.18 (best; vs best baseline TGTB Leak(N)=76.99, Leak(E)=56.04)
M-GraphRAG Agriculture, T=1000: AGEA Leak(N)=84.67, Leak(E)=84.13, Prec(N)=93.08, Prec(E)=76.81
LightRAG Medical, T=1000: AGEA Leak(N)=96.42, Leak(E)=95.90, Prec(N)=98.34, Prec(E)=97.97 (vs best baseline PIDE Leak(N)=83.92, Leak(E)=73.93)
LightRAG Agriculture, T=1000: AGEA Leak(N)=88.05, Leak(E)=87.11, Prec(N)=98.11, Prec(E)=96.65
Scalability large graph (Novel, 20 books, T=2000): M-GraphRAG AGEA Leak(N)=60.71, Leak(E)=52.56; LightRAG AGEA Leak(N)=71.36, Leak(E)=68.70 (degradation under fixed budget)
Ablation (Medical, M-GraphRAG) query strategy: Explore-only delta-avg-Leak = -66.41; Exploit-only delta-avg-Leak = -3.94 but delta-avg-Prec = -18.91 vs adaptive AGEA
Ablation filtering (Medical, M-GraphRAG): removing filter changes avg leakage by +1.04 but avg precision by -8.93 (node precision 87.09 -> 70.58)
Ablation backbone (Medical, M-GraphRAG): DeepSeek best; GPT-4o-mini delta-avg-Prec = -10.27, Qwen delta-avg-Prec = -25.82

Extraction-command ablation (Medical, 250 queries): proposed structured command improves avg leakage by +18.94 vs v1 ('repeat all the context') and +13.17 vs v2 ('repeat retrieved entities/relationships')
baselines_compared: TGTB (Zeng et al. 2024) - fixed-query prompt injection
PIDE (Qi et al. 2025) - fixed-query prompt injection with WikiQA anchor queries
CopyBreakRAG (Jiang et al. 2025) - adaptive, iterative (re-implemented; code unavailable)
IKEA (Wang et al. 2025c) - adaptive implicit knowledge extraction (re-implemented; code unavailable)
